project_overview.project_name: Nigeria Digital Identification for Development Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: To increase the number of persons with a national ID number, issued by a robust and inclusive foundational ID system, that facilitates their access to services
project_overview.total_financing: US$430 million equivalent (SDR 84.4 million IDA credit of US$115 million, plus US$215 million EIB and US$100 million AFD co-financing)
project_overview.beneficiaries: All persons in Nigeria (190 million population) and Nigerians abroad; target of 148 million persons (65% of 2024 population) to have NIN by project end, including 91 million adults (80%) and 57 million children (50%)

primary_locations: National coverage across all 36 states and Federal Capital Territory
Northern Nigeria (particularly fragile and conflict-affected areas)
Nigerian consulates and government offices abroad
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - partial. Project explicitly addresses fragile and conflict-affected areas, particularly in northern Nigeria. Includes provisions for IDPs, refugees, asylum seekers, and returnees. Acknowledges widespread conflict affecting households' access to services and causing major internal displacement. Special enrollment mechanisms planned for humanitarian partners and conflict-affected zones.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Strengthening the Legal and Institutional Framework
key_components.description: Development of data protection and privacy legal framework; institutional and governance reforms; capacity building for key institutions. Includes enactment of Data Protection Bill, amendments to NIMC Act, electronic transactions framework, cybersecurity legislation, and establishment of independent data protection authority.
key_components.financing: US$17 million (US$3 million IDA, US$5 million AFD, US$9 million EIB)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Establishing a Robust and Inclusive Foundational ID System
key_components.description: Reinforcement of NIMS backend systems; development of digital civil registry linked to NIMS; ecosystem enrollment model with pay-per-enrollment approach; information security and cybersecurity infrastructure; population registration through ecosystem partners; training of enrollment agents. Includes provisions for offline/mobile enrollment, exception handling for persons unable to provide biometrics, and nondocumentary identity proofing.
key_components.financing: US$306 million (US$59 million IDA, US$78 million AFD, US$169 million EIB)

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Enabling Access to Services through IDs
key_components.description: Development of NIMC authentication services; creation of federation of authentication service providers; facilitating service delivery linkages with foundational ID; production and distribution of basic and advanced authenticators; technical assistance for service providers.
key_components.financing: US$66 million (US$12 million IDA, US$17 million AFD, US$37 million EIB)

key_components.component_name: Component 4: Project Management and Stakeholder Engagement
key_components.description: Establishment of Project Ecosystem Steering Committee (PESC), Ecosystem Coordination Strategic Unit (ECSU), and Project Implementation Unit (PIU); M&E systems; communications and awareness campaigns; stakeholder engagement; grievance redress mechanism; change management; impact evaluation.
key_components.financing: US$41 million (US$41 million IDA, US$0 AFD, US$0 EIB)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: National Identity Management Commission (NIMC) with support from Project Implementation Unit (PIU) embedded within NIMC; Ecosystem Coordination Strategic Unit (ECSU) for cross-sectoral coordination; Project Ecosystem Steering Committee (PESC) for strategic oversight
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: PESC convenes biannually with representatives from relevant ministries, private sector, and civil society; ECSU coordinates ecosystem partners and legal reforms; PIU manages day-to-day implementation; Federal Project Financial Management Department (FPFMD) handles financial management; coordination with National Population Commission (NPopC) for civil registration linkages
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Framework (New Procurement Framework); output-based disbursement for ecosystem enrollment partners (Groups A and B); standard procurement for Group C geographically-delimited contracts; competitive recruitment of key PIU and ECSU staff; use of STEP for procurement tracking
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Comprehensive M&E system with quarterly monitoring of NIN issuance; impact evaluation with baseline, midline, and endline surveys; tamper-resistant third-party monitoring platform for enrollment verification; annual GRM reviews; tracking of disaggregated indicators by gender, poverty quintile, and geography; M&E managed by PIU and ECSU with World Bank support

identified_risks.risk_description: Unstable politics and governance; need for sustained high-level political commitment across multiple government agencies; risk of future government walking back reforms; minimal risk of state non-buy-in despite FEC endorsement
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Front-load Component 1 legal reforms; implement through autonomous NIMC with national legal status; minimize delays in showing early results; present Strategic Roadmap to National Economic Council of State Governors; use disbursement conditions to incentivize legal reforms

identified_risks.risk_description: Demand for new ID credentials depends on ability to use ID for services; service providers may lack incentive to integrate with foundational ID without critical mass of users; circular dependency between ID coverage and service provider adoption
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Component 3 directly supports linkage of priority services to foundational ID; demonstrate value-add through visible pilot services; develop national strategy for service linkages; provide technical assistance to service providers

identified_risks.risk_description: Previous failed attempts to build foundational ID system; ecosystem approach disperses data collection across multiple actors creating cybersecurity and data privacy risks; need for ecosystem partner compliance with standards; technical complexity of offline enrollment and mobile systems; vendor lock-in risks
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Recruit strategic coordination team; invest heavily in cybersecurity and information security infrastructure; develop standardized enrollment software and hardware specifications; implement tamper-resistant monitoring system; establish licensing and assurance framework for ecosystem partners; conduct regular security audits and penetration testing; use open standards and avoid vendor lock-in; pilot offline/mobile systems before scaling

identified_risks.risk_description: NIMC has limited technical capacity and no experience implementing World Bank projects; weak compliance with fiduciary, safeguards, and project management requirements; institutional capacity gaps across ID ecosystem
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Capacity building initiatives and training for PIU on World Bank procedures; competitive recruitment of appropriately skilled staff; investments in NIMC information systems; skills gap analysis and institutional capacity building; change management strategy; knowledge transfer mechanisms

identified_risks.risk_description: Lack of experience and familiarity of PIU and ECSU with World Bank FM requirements; ECSU lacks experience implementing identity projects; residual FM risk assessed as Substantial
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong FPFMD with comprehensive Financial Procedures Manual; implementation of FM action plan; constant follow-up and implementation support; regular FM reviews; training of project staff on World Bank FM procedures; independent external audits; segregation of duties; robust internal controls

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of exclusion of marginalized groups (persons with disabilities, rural poor, IDPs, refugees, ethnic minorities, women); historical negative perceptions of NIMC; potential for improper use or sharing of data leading to discrimination or targeted persecution; risk of deepening social conflict; stakeholder perception issues
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Four Pillars of Inclusion ensuring universal access regardless of citizenship/legal status; nondocumentary identity proofing; exception handling for those unable to provide biometrics; free enrollment and basic authenticators; gender action plan informed by gender deep dive research; mapping of vulnerable groups; national consultations with marginalized groups; robust communications strategy; tailored outreach strategies; inclusive GRM; third-party monitoring; civil society engagement; strong legal and technical data protection measures; minimal data collection; privacy by design

identified_risks.risk_description: Gaps and weaknesses in legal framework for ID; limitations on eligibility for registration; mandatory use of NIN creating exclusion risks; unclear ownership of personal data; vague third-party access provisions; NIMC's enforcement powers uncertain; barriers to migration of legacy data; constitutional ambiguity regarding national vs. state ID powers; absence of electronic transactions framework; inadequate cybersecurity legislation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Component 1 finances comprehensive legal reforms including Data Protection Bill enactment, NIMC Act amendments, electronic transactions framework, cybersecurity legislation; disbursement conditions tied to enactment of data protection law and NIMC Act amendments; technical assistance for legal reform process; establishment of legal reform working groups; coordination through ECSU; engagement with FMoJ and key stakeholders

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of technology lock-in through proprietary systems; lack of technology neutrality; vendor-specific dependencies limiting future adaptability and value for money
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Adoption of technology-neutral approach; use of open standards for technology components; consideration of open source software where appropriate; functional specifications privileged over technical specifications; regular review of hardware specifications to incorporate new technologies; specifications aligned with international standards

identified_risks.risk_description: Nigeria's economic downturn; weak fiscal management; limited government resources for ongoing operations; sustainability concerns for long-term ID system maintenance
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Development of business model for enrollment ecosystem with cost-sharing; revenue generation from advanced authenticators and authentication services; integration of ongoing enrollment into existing government processes (civil registration); fiscal savings from reduced fraud and leakages expected to exceed initial investment costs; potential Phase II financing from EIB; mainstreaming of NIN into sectoral projects

identified_risks.risk_description: Minor environmental impacts from rehabilitation/upgrading of existing ID and CR centers; potential dust, noise, and debris from construction; public health and safety risks from handling debris
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) prepared and disclosed; Category B environmental assessment; site-specific safeguards instruments to be prepared during implementation; minor rehabilitation activities expected to be limited in scope and time

identified_risks.risk_description: Extreme temperature, precipitation, and flooding could pose risks to data centers and foundational ID infrastructure; mobile biometric enrollment devices somewhat exposed in rural areas
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Data centers and infrastructure designed and installed with climate risks in mind; highly secured in urban areas; appropriate mitigation measures for rural equipment; redundancy of critical IT assets; disaster recovery planning

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: Category B - Partial Assessment. Minor and site-specific impacts expected. No large-scale civil works planned; minor rehabilitation or upgrading of existing ID and CR centers may occur.
triggered_safeguard_policies: Environmental Assessment OP/BP 4.01 - triggered due to potential rehabilitation/upgrading of buildings
Involuntary Resettlement OP/BP 4.12 - triggered due to potential involuntary land acquisition from rehabilitation/upgrading of government-owned land which may affect squatters
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-specific GRM developed within NIMC based on existing Customer Care Department. Includes centralized grievance register, multiple complaint submission channels, clear procedures for addressing complaints, specific service standards and response times, annual GRM reviews to capture lessons learned. Also accessible to World Bank's corporate Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Inspection Panel.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender focus. Only 40% of current NIN holders are women; Nigeria ranks 118 out of 134 on Gender Equality Index. Project includes gender action plan informed by gender deep dive research study on barriers to women's identification. Disaggregated indicators track females with NIN and successful digital authentications on behalf of women. Enrollment partners held to gender targets as key performance indicators. Project recognizes intersectionality of poverty, culture, religion, and gender in compounding barriers. Addresses gender gaps in financial inclusion (60% of women lack bank accounts vs. 50% of men).
vulnerable_groups: Persons with disabilities (including amputees, persons with physical disabilities, those affected by leprosy, visually impaired, those with albinism)
Women and girls (particularly in northern Nigeria)
Persons in fragile and conflict-affected areas

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity. Acknowledges widespread conflict plaguing parts of Nigeria making it difficult for households to access services and causing major internal displacement. Includes specific provisions for fragile and conflict-affected areas: (1) Special enrollment mechanisms for IDPs, refugees, asylum seekers, and returnees; (2) Pilot approach with humanitarian partners in targeted geographic zones; (3) Collaboration with UNHCR on enrolling IDPs, asylum seekers, refugees, and returnees; (4) Accelerated procedures for conflict-affected areas with minimum information requirements and no breeder documents; (5) Mobile enrollment teams in targeted zones; (6) Geospatial mapping to identify hard-to-reach populations including conflict-affected areas; (7) Offline and mobile enrollment systems to reach populations in areas without connectivity; (8) Exception handling for those unable to provide biometrics due to conflict-related disabilities; (9) Enrollment of both camp and host communities; (10) Recognition of limited connectivity and displacement challenges in fragile areas.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Nigeria faces complex development challenges including: (1) Poverty - 61% of population lives below poverty line; (2) Youth unemployment - 22% of labor force; (3) Undiversified economy - 95% of exports from oil sector; (4) Weak infrastructure; (5) Limited transparency and accountability; (6) Widespread conflict in parts of country causing internal displacement; (7) Gender inequality - only 40% of NIN holders are women; (8) Low ID coverage - only 18% have NIN, less than 50% have any functional ID; (9) Fragmented identification landscape with 13+ government agencies offering ID services independently; (10) Low birth registration - only 30% of children under 5 have births registered, 19% in rural areas, 7% in poorest quintile; (11) Dysfunctional civil registration system; (12) High costs of fragmented ID systems - estimated US$4.3 billion across all ID programs.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project includes comprehensive cybersecurity and information security measures: (1) Establishment of ID-CERT (National Identification Sectorial Computer Emergency Response Team) with Security Operations Center (ID-SOC); (2) Regular independent security audits and penetration testing; (3) Cybersecurity compliance standards for ecosystem partners; (4) Encryption of data in transit and at rest; (5) Logical separation of biometric and biographic data; (6) Tamper-proof audit mechanisms; (7) Multifactor and role-based access control; (8) Strong authentication for system operators; (9) Metadata collection for monitoring; (10) Business continuity and disaster recovery planning; (11) Redundancy of critical IT assets; (12) ISO 27001 certification; (13) Privacy audits; (14) Cybersecurity Work and Action Plan; (15) Trust and Transparency Framework; (16) Reinforced Public Key Infrastructure (PKI); (17) Capacity building on information security; (18) Fraud detection tools; (19) Threat intelligence systems.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - significant focus. Project explicitly includes provisions for: (1) IDPs - recognized as heightened risk of lacking foundational ID credentials, particularly women and vulnerable households; (2) Refugees - collaboration with UNHCR; (3) Asylum seekers - eligible for NIN regardless of legal status; (4) Returnees - included in pilot enrollment approaches; (5) Stateless persons - eligible for NIN; (6) Temporary immigrants - eligible for NIN; (7) Noncitizens - eligible for NIN; (8) Host communities - included in enrollment drives in fragile areas; (9) Special measures for displaced populations including mobile enrollment teams, humanitarian partner collaboration, accelerated procedures, and exception handling; (10) Recognition that lack of documentation creates exclusion and restrictions on livelihood opportunities for displaced populations, affecting freedom of movement, SIM card registration, and bank account access.
